channel.title: math on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in math on mahmood

item.title: forward chaining
item.description: a family of inference algorithms propositional logic chaining the forward-chaining algorithm (which stands for “propositional logic forward chaining entails” and is shown in 1) determines if a single proposition symbol –the query–is entailed by a knowledge base of definite clauses. it begins from known facts (positive literals) in the knowledge base. if all the premises of an implication are known, then its conclusion is added to the set of known facts.

item.title: logic programming in common lisp
item.description: this document needs some polishing!, the backward chaining algorithm isnt finished for example logic programming in common lisp logical statements we make use of the symbolic capabilities of lisp, we use quoting and symbols to represent logical statements, e.g. given the following knowledge base of horn clauses: in lisp we could write it as: (defparameter *kb* '((implies p q) (implies (and l m) p) (implies (and b l) m) (implies (and a p) l) (implies (and a b s) l) A B)) *kb* ((IMPLIES P Q) (IMPLIES (AND L M) P) (IMPLIES (AND B L) M) (IMPLIES (AND A P) L) (IMPLIES (AND A B S) L) A B) the choice of the symbols implies, and could vary, but essentially we should write code that deals properly with these symbols based on their names.

item.title: unification
item.description: the algorithm (shown in 1) takes two sentences and returns a unifier for them (a substitution) if one exists: let us look at some examples of how should behave. suppose we have a query : whom does John know? answers to this query can be found by finding all sentences in the knowledge base that unify with . here are the results of unification with four different sentences that might be in the knowledge base: the last unification fails because cannot take on the values John and Elizabeth at the same time.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 4
item.description: all the code used here can be found at https://mahmoodsheikh36.github.io assume you have the following knowledge create a decision tree based on the knowledge base above (ignore direction). the graph with the electric layout algorithm: (defparameter *homework4-knowledge-base* '((implies (and a b ) e ) (implies (and a c ) k ) (implies (and a c d) f ) (implies (and c d ) g ) (implies (and e f ) h ) (implies (and f g ) i ) (implies (and f e g) j ) (implies (and k l ) (and m n)) (implies (and f d ) l ) a b c)) (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :node-distance 60) the same graph with the layered layout algorithm: (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :tikz-params "layered layout, grow=up, edges={-{Stealth[]}}, edges=rounded corners, level sep=40px") using backward chaining, try to prove using , and as the data.

item.title: learning math functions with neural networks
item.description: see polynomial regression quick demo linear regression i could’ve used my code for multivariate linear regression but i wanna study how neural networks can do regression tasks if we were to try approximating a linear function using a simple multilayer perceptron we would see that since most activation functions squash the values of the network into a small range like in case of the sigmoid, it would be hard to regress to functions whose codomain is different that that of the activation functions, so we probably want to use a special activation function, or even easier, drop activation functions altogether, this way the neural network would basically be doing linear regression lets begin by trying to learn a linear function, for that we need to be able to generate random linear functions, perhaps with some noise, so we write a simple function for that: (defun generate-points-along-function (fn noise-variance n &optional (range 1000)) "generate n points along a function with some random noise.

item.title: constraint satisfaction problem
item.description: refer to [chapter 6] a constraint satisfaction problem consists of three components, , , and : is a set of variables, is a set of domains, , one for each variable is a set of constraints that specify allowable combinations of values a domain, , consists of a set of allowable values, , for variable . for example, a boolean variable would have the domain . different variables can have different domains of different sizes.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 3
item.description: scroll to the end if you’re only interested in the solution and the steps in raw text.. homework on constraint satisfaction problem using common lisp graphs graph image from homework: gotta solve this as an rgba map coloring problem make use of forward checking prioritize vertices with least constraining (least edges, least constraints) to most constraining (most edges), in case of a draw, use the variable with the most remaining colors (least constrained), if there is another tie, choose the vertex to the left first we define the graph: (defparameter *mygraph* (make-graph :adjacency-list '((a .

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 2
item.description: given the following game tree original tree image from homework: implement pruning, show the optimal tree and the pruned subtrees (explain) a step by step execution, at each step the modified node and the nodes that it depends on to determine its value are highlighted red, the assumption here is that the depth-first search algorithm prefers the left side (applies recursion to left node first, then right node), next to each node written are the values of for the state associated with it, the pruned nodes are colored blue.

item.title: dijkstra's algorithm
item.description: an algorithm that finds the minimum distances from a given “origin” vertex to all the other vertices in a weighted graph relaxing step: the method is the building block of dijkstra’s algorithm, it checks whether the path we’ve taken to get to the vertex we’re currently at is shorter than a past path we’ve taken (the length of the past path at that time would be stored in , being the distance array and being the vertex we’ve arrived at), and resets the value of and accordingly.

item.title: convolution
item.description: (Maria Vakalopoulou, Stergios Christodoulidis, Ninon Burgos, Olivier Colliot, Vincent Lepetit. Deep learning: basics and convolutional neural networks (CNN). Olivier Colliot. Machine Learning for Brain Disorders, Springer, In press. hal-03957224, 2023) convolution: the convolution operation is defined as the integral of the product of two functions after one is reflected about the y-axis and shifted: in essence, the convolution operation shows how one function affects the other discrete convolution: for two functions , the discrete convolution of and is given by: extending to 2 dimensional functions we’d get: in machine learning, specifically in convolutional neural network, the convolution operation is used do detect features in an image, one of the operands is the image and the second is the kernel, see 1 although the kernel has to be rotated around every axis before we begin sliding it over the image, otherwise we’d be doing cross-correlation instead of convolution here is an example of a flipped kernel: padding: if you look at 1, notice the edges essentially get “trimmed off”, converting a 7x7 matrix into a 5×5 one.

item.title: bipartite graph
item.description: let be an undirected graph, the graph is bipartite if and for each edge , and we denote a bipartite graph by

item.title: edmonds-karp algorithm
item.description: an algorithm for computing the maximum flow in a flow network in time algorithm unwritten length of a flow augmenting path: we define the length of a flow augmenting path as the number of edges in it the time complexity of this algorithm is residual distance: given a residual network we define a residual distance as the number of edges in the minimal path between and if we run the algorithm on a residual network then for each vertex , the distance from to , , becomes greater after each flow improvement.

item.title: ford–fulkerson algorithm
item.description: the algorithm finds the flow function with the maximal total flow in a given flow network once the algorithm is done, in the network there would be a saturated s-t cut the time complexity of this algorithm depends on the way we pick the flow augmenting paths the time complexity is

item.title: maximum bipartite matching
item.description: given the graph the set of edges represents a matching if no two edges in are adjacent a matching in is maximal if for every other matching in , its true that there could be multiple maximum bipartite matchings in a given graph here we propose an algorithm that finds a maximum bipartite matching in a given bipartite graph, but first we propose an algorithm that turns a given bipartite graph into a flow network now we propose an algorithm which finds a maximum bipartite matching in which depends on which is the flow network received from applying the above algorithm to let be a bipartite graph, and let be a flow network that corresponds to .

item.title: flow network
item.description: a flow network consists of: a directed loopless graph with no parallel edges for each edge a capacity function is defined such that an origin vertex , such that a destination vertex , such that the flow in any edge isnt higher than its capacity for each vertex : i.e. the sum of flows in the edges that enter is equal to that of those that exit total flow of the network: the total flow in a network is defined as the sum of flows that enter the destination vertex: the following figure represents a flow network:

item.title: linearly mapping an interval onto another
item.description: basically the idea is that, given an real interval and the number such that and another interval , we wanna find the number such that we need to isolate : extending to higher dimensions assume a vector space with a dimension of , given the 2 sets of real intervals and a vector : where for all is bound in the interval , i.e. we wanna find the vector : such that so we write the linear transformation (might not be necessarily linear but affine) obviously this function doesnt preserve the origin as , so its an affine transformation, we separate the intercept so we can drop it and add it later: we drop the intercept and find the transformation matrix of the function without it, which is a square matrix of size im taking it for granted that the without the intercept is linear, in reality we need to check if the properties of a linear function are preserved in then we add the intercept with homogeneous coordinates to get the final matrix, which is a square matrix of size so to conclude, the formula transforms the vector from a given set of intervals onto another, resulting in

item.title: 2d transformations
item.description: we usually use a vector to represent a point in 2d space a general form of linear transformations (or more precisely, affine transformations) in can be wrriten as: or or, in matrix form, using homogeneous coordinates: matrices allow arbitrary linear transformations to be displayed in a consistent format, suitable for computation 2d translation given a point , we wanna reposition it with the translation distance this is an affine transformation, we deduce an affine transformation matrix: 2d rotation about the origin a rotation about the origin by angle can be done with the matrix: 2d scaling scaling is just multiplying the variables by some constants, it is defined using the cross product of the point by the scaling vector we deduce the transformation matrix: using homogeneous coordinates: linearly mapping a grid to another simplifying the formula from linearly mapping an interval onto another to 2 dimensions: mapping the point from the intervals , respectively, to the intervals , respectively

item.title: biconnected component
item.description: a biconnected component is a biconnected subgraph that isnt a subgraph of another biconnected subgraph in the same graph. algorithm for detecting biconnected components we use a modified version of the dfs algorithm to detect biconnected components in a given graph, but first we need to propose some lemmas and principles to help us we propose 3 lemmas that the algorithm uses to determine whether a vertex of a certain type is a separation vertex the lemma for detecting whether the root is a separation vertex: let be a dfs tree and let be the root of , is a separation vertex iff its out-degree is greater than 1 the lemma for detecting whether a leaf is a separation vertex: a leaf of a dfs tree isnt a separation vertex the lemma for detecting whether an internal node is a separation vertex: let be an internal node (that isnt the root) in the dfs tree .

item.title: breadth-first search
item.description: an algorithm that finds the distances from a given vertex to all the other vertices in an unweighted undirected connected graph the time complexity of is . after the execution of , the algorithm goes once through all the vertices, and twice (in an undirected graph) or once (in a directed graph) through every edge. the time complexity of is for each vertex , the values of the variables are changed once at most during the execution of the algorithm.

item.title: depth-first search
item.description: depth-first search is an algorithm for traversing graphs. dfs starts at an arbitrary vertex and progresses from neighbor to neighbor. a free vertex is one that hasnt yet been visited during the traversal, two timestamps are set for each vertex, discovery timestamp and departure timestamp, each timestamp is a number between 1 and . each such number corresponds to only one timestamp, the timestamps are stored in the following variables: discovery time: which is set when is first visited during the traversal departure time: which is set when is last departured next to each vertex is its discovery/departure timestamps Figure 1: an example of dfs execution

item.title: convolutional neural network
item.description: this is still very much a work in progress some of the math notation may be inconsistent, but things are valid and make sense when read in context (i should fix that though) citations (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) (Jianxin Wu, 2017) (Zhifei Zhang, 2016) and countless other resources that helped me form a good understanding of the topics related to conv networks motivation an image cannot be thought of as a simple vector of input pixel values, primarily because adjacency of pixels in an image matters.

item.title: kruskal's algorithm
item.description: could be seen as an alternative to prim’s algorithm as they generally serve the same goal kruskals algorithm finds an mst in a graph initial (inefficient) proposed algorithm: correctness of kruskal: at each step during the execution of , the forest is a subgraph of an mst of the input graph. the lemma represents a loop invariant that is true at the start of each iteration of the for loop. we prove the lemma by induction on the loop index.

item.title: floyd-warshall algorithm
item.description: an algorithm to find the distance matrix of a directed weighted graph let be a weighted graph with the set of vertices we denote by the induced subgraph by the vertices for every pair of vertices we denote by the induced subgraph by the vertices for every pair of vertices, we denote by one of the minimal paths (if it exists) from to in for every pair of vertices if in there exists a minimal path from to , , we write if in there exists a path from to that contains a negative cycle we write if in there isnt a path from to we write for every if in there isnt a negative cycle that touches then for every if in there is a negative cycle that touches then for every , (if it exists) is the shortest path from to without any vertices in between, meaning the path is just the edge for every , (if it exists) is the shortest path from to , i.

item.title: prim's algorithm
item.description: a greedy algorithm to find an mst in a given undirected weighted graph. a basic implementation (better complexity can be achieved with a better implementation, proposed later in this note): at each step of prim’s algorithm the graph is a tree it follows trivially from the code. is also a subgraph of an mst of proof by induction on the number of iterations in the loop base step: immediately after initialization, , at this point, the graph contains a single arbitrary vertex of and contains no edges, each mst of contains all vertices in , so the claim is correct in this case.

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.

item.title: minimum spanning tree
item.description: given an undirected, connected and weighted graph . let be a spanning tree of . the weight of is the sum of weights of its edges. i.e. the tree is a minimum spanning tree if for every other spanning tree of , the following is true: a graph may have multiple mst’s let be an undirected simple non-negatively weighted graph. let be a simple cycle in . let be the edge with the absolutely biggest weight in the cycle .

item.title: feedforward neural network
item.description: feedforward neural networks is a wider term than discussed here, whats discussed here really is a specific type we call a simple multilayer perceptron, but this serves as an introductory to other types of more complex networks a feedforward network has connections only in one direction. each node computes a function of its inputs and passes the result to its successors in the network. information flows through the network from the input nodes to the output nodes, and there are no loops.

item.title: algorithm correctness
item.description: an algorithm is totally correct if it receives valid input, terminates, and always returns the correct output the only way to prove the correctness of an algorithm over all possible inputs is by reasoning formally or mathematically about it. generally, to prove an algorithm is correct we prove two things the algorithm terminates the algorithm returns the correct value for every input we propose a statement that describes what the algorithm takes as input and what its supposed to output and then prove it proving algorithm correctness by induction here we’ll be using mathematical induction for each input that is an array of numbers A, the algorithm computes the sum of the numbers proof the algorithm terminates: the algorithm consists of a for loop that is bound by n, so it follows trivially that it must terminate proof of correctness by perfect induction on the length of the input array we propose the following loop invariant for the loop in this case, proving the proposed loop invariant would prove the algorithm correct for , which is the base case, the algorithm returns A[1] which would be the only element in the array, so its obvious that its the correct output the induction hypothesis, we assume after i iterations in the loop that the induction step, during the ‘th iteration of the loop, we add to the variable sum, and so it follows from this and from the induction hypothesis that: the loop runs n times, by substituting n in the loop invariant, we’d get which means that after the loop is done, the variable sum would contain the sum of the elements in the array, which is what the function returns, which is the correct output QED correctness of recursive algorithms the pattern for proofs of recursive algorithms is as follows: base step of the recursion, we have to manually show that for each of the edge cases the output is correct induction hypothesis, we assume that in each recurring call the output is correct proof, we show that the output is correct for the main function call consider the following function proof that the algorithm terminates: assume that runs on an array A, on each recurring call, the size of the input array A is halved, its obvious that at the end of every sequence of calls the size of the input array would arrive to 1 which is the edge case that would cause the function to terminate proof of correctness: we prove this by induction on the number of elements in the array base case: for which is the edge case, the algorithm returns , the only element in , its obvious that the output is correct induction hypothesis: we use perfect induction on the size of the input, we assume that the algorithm correctly computes the sum of the elements of any array whose size is smaller than n induction step: let A be an arbitrary array of size n, the call to executes the calls and , the size of the arrays and is smaller than , so according to the previous step we know those two recurring calls do return the correct sum of the corresponding subarrays, and since we are returning the sum of those sums we know for sure we are returning the correct sum

item.title: array as map algorithm
item.description: using an array as a lookup table consider two vectors, of sizes , we wanna check whether each number in is in what does the following algorithm do? (|A|=N) the time complexity is such that

item.title: bellman-ford algorithm
item.description: is used to find distances and negative cycles in directed graphs the difference between dijkstra’s algorithm and bellman-ford is that bellman works on all real-valued weights and can handle negative weights while dijkstra cant consider the following graph whose weights function is taken from the real number line

item.title: decision tree
item.description: (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a “decision”–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: dynamic programming
item.description: dynamic programming amounts to breaking down an optimization problem into simpler sub-problems, and storing the solution to each sub-problem so that each sub-problem is only solved once. to be honest, this definition may not make total sense until you see an example of a sub-problem org-roam couldnt handle link 86EDA56E-8196-42EA-9EDA-D7ECFC5A076B::dynamic_programming_essential_problem_1, error was: (wrong-type-argument org-roam-node nil) provide an algorithm that is as efficient as possible that solves the following problem: input: an array of natural numbers output: a list of k pairs () of indicies of the form i,i+1 such that none of the indicies of the array appears more than once and the sum is maximal example: for the input 12,11,2,4,1,10,8,12 the output should be (11,2),(4,1),(10,8) write an algorithm to find the pairs of indicies as well as the sum describe the algorithm in words its all in the next subquestion write pseudocode for the algorithm first we propose a solution, then optimize it using dynamic programming to make this simpler for implementation and for further optimization we rewrite the algorithm and use an additional argument, , thats an index whose initial value should be , because thats how the algorithm i’ve written works this algorithm has exponential time complexity, we could precompute the values that would be returned by into an array B, such that , we use another array C to keep track of the pairs we pick, only because we are required to do so by running , would contain the pairs of indicies and the sum we’re looking for, this algorithm runs in there is something i couldnt get quite right with i think this is wrong, there is something wrong with the way im keeping record of the pairs in but im too lazy/exhausted to figure it out we try another approach, we use an array B_w such that B_w[i] is defined as the solution to the problem in the space such that A[i] takes part in the sum and another array Bwo such that Bwo[i] is defined as the solution to the problem in the space such that A[i] doesnt take part in the sum this solution is probably wrong aswell.

item.title: graph
item.description: a graph is an ordered pair , where is a set whose elements are called vertices, and is a set of paired vertices, whose elements are called edges consider the graph

item.title: greedy algorithm
item.description: the method of greediness proposes that in each step in an algorithm, where we are presented with multiple choices, the choice with the biggest instantaneous gain is picked using this method we can write algorithm that, while relatively simple, arent usually optimal input: an array A of positive numbers output: a subarray (non-consecutive) whose sum is maximal which doesnt contain two consecutive elements the algorithm picks the biggest number in the input array, adds it to the total sum, and discards the two neighboring elements but is this algorithm correct?

item.title: recurrence relation
item.description: to analyze the time complexity of recursive functions we use a mathematical concept called recurrence relation a recurrence defines in terms of for smaller values , here is defined in terms of

item.title: select algorithm
item.description: an algorithm that would help us reduce the time complexity of many problems input: an array of numbers and a normal number such that output: the element in whose rank is , i.e. the ‘th smallest number in example: for the input , the output is 13 a naive approach: the correctness of this algorithm trivially follows from the definition of the given problem we propose a more afficient algorithm that we’ll call which solves the problem in linear time: given an array of different numbers, and a number , , let be a number such that assume that the partition algorithm was applied to and we got the subarrays of sizes if then is in and if then is in and for example, given the array we’d get in there are the 5 smallest numbers from , the sixth smallest number in A, 8, is the minimal number in the correctness of the algorithm follows trivially from the lemma we are sacrificing linear time finding a good pivot so that the partition algorithm’s worst-case performance doesnt reach , which may happen when the size of one of the partitions happens to be 1 repeatedly this algorithm runs in linear time a company has employees, their salaries differ and are given in an array , it wants to give its employees bonuses (it wants to spend as much as it can, given a limited budget), a bonus is the same amount as ones salary, given , the sum of bonuses is less or equal to a given number , solve the problem in the recurrence formula for this algorithm is which simplifies to

item.title: selection sort
item.description: selection sort maintains two subarrays in a given array, one subarray that is sorted and the other unsorted, it sorts a given array by repeatedly finding the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted part and putting it at the beginning in every iteration of selection sort, the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted subarray is picked and moved to the sorted subarray. time complexity is template <typename T> void selection_sort(T arr[], int len) { for (int i = 0; i < len; ++i) { // find index of minimum in unsorted subarray int min_idx = i; for (int j = i+1; j < len; ++j) { if (arr[j] < arr[min_idx]) { min_idx = j; } } // add minimum to the sorted subarray T tmp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[min_idx]; arr[min_idx] = tmp; } } example usage: #include <iostream> int main() { int arr[] = {8, 1, 2, 10, 50, 18}; selection_sort(arr, std::size(arr)); for (int i = 0; i < std::size(arr); ++i) { std::cout << arr[i] << std::endl; } } 1 2 8 10 18 50

item.title: time complexity
item.description: the definitions here are proper let P be a computational problem, the complexity of P is the time complexity of the fastest possible algorithm that solves P the time complexity of an algorithm is defined as the amount of time taken by it to run, as a function of the length of the input let P be a computational problem, let be a function over the normal numbers, to prove the time complexity of P to be equal to we have to show that: there exists an algorithm that solves P whose time complexity is the time complexity of every algorithm that solves P is atleast , i.

item.title: discrete math
item.description: set subset set A is a subset of a set B if all elements of A are also elements of B union intersection disjoint sets two sets are said to be disjoint sets if they have no elements in common multiset a multiset is a modification of the concept of a set that, unlike a set, allows for multiple instances for each of its elements. the number of instances given for each element is called the multiplicity of that element in the multiset ordered pair given are mathematical objects, is an ordered pair, , as an example, the coordinates on a function is an ordered pair cartesian product cartesian product of 2 sets is the set of all the possible ordered pairs that can be obtained by taking an element from as the first in the pair and an element from as the second assume the cartesian product of where is: assume power: or or assume or or need to prove: we split into cases: case 1: assume case 2: assume , same as case 3: assume assume need to prove: or or we assume in contradiction that and and exists an so that and or there exists an so that and we split into cases: case 1: there exists an so that and exists so that and there exists an so that and there exists so that and case 2: there exists so that and , a similar path to the previous case we arrived at a contradiction so the theorem is true relation assuming and are sets, if then is a relation from to binary relation a relation is a binary relation if over the field reflexive relation let be a relation over set we say is reflexive if for every its true that (or we can write ) isnt reflexive there exists so that matrices that represent reflexive relations are always in echelon form, e.

item.title: linear algebra
item.description: this document needs some refactoring, some of the proofs may be incomplete i also need to divide this document into smaller notes that can be read independently of each other, since the order here is messed up anyway and it makes no sense to use a progressive layout field placeholder.. matrix a matrix is a rectangular array or table of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns, which is used to represent a mathematical object or a property of such an object a matrix can be constructed over a specific finite/infinite set of numbers for example which is the set of integers, when we say a matrix is over a specific field of numbers we mean that the numbers in the matrix are limited to that field of numbers consider this matrix over the field , where all the numbers are integers matrix multiplication let be matrices matrix multiplication is possible if and only if the matrices have the dimensions and the result matrix is defined by matrices can be multiplied by constants, let be a matrix and be a constant, the definition of such multiplication is: matrix addition matrix addition is defined as the addition of 2 matrices with the same dimensions elementary row operation augmented matrix for a given system of equation, an augmented matrix is the system of equations represented using a matrix square matrix a square matrix is a matrix whose width is the same as its height, i.
